metadata.purpose: Climbing, trailing, creeping, and mat-forming plants are stored here by stable growth form, regardless of which fruit, leaf, flower, or underground part is useful.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Trailing evergreen shrub of dry sandy places with leathery leaves and red berries.
records.culinary_uses: Red berries are edible though mealy. Leaf tea is treated here as traditional medicinal use, not ordinary beverage tea.
records.edibility_detail: Fruit is edible but mealy and not usually a prime fresh-eating berry. Leaf tea belongs to medicinal/traditional use, not normal edible-beverage routing.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs. Fruit is edible; leaf tea is traditional medicinal use, not ordinary beverage tea in this guide.
records.plant_card_note: Fruit is edible though mealy. Leaf tea is traditional medicinal use, not ordinary beverage tea in this guide.

images_structured.author: Matt Lavin from Bozeman, Montana, USA

records.source_note: Source links added for identification/range context; food-use wording remains modest and caution-framed where appropriate.

records.notes: Low woodland dogwood with whorled leaves, white dogwood-like bracts, and clusters of red berries later in season.
records.culinary_uses: Red berries are edible though mild and are more of a trail nibble or mixed-berry addition than a heavy-yield fruit.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional northern use exists, though it is more often treated as a woodland edible than a major medicinal species.

images_structured.title: Bunchberry colony in flower
images_structured.author: Fungus Guy
images_structured.credit: Fungus Guy, public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
images_structured.rights_notes: Verified from Wikimedia Commons: author Fungus Guy released the work into the public domain. Two underdocumented Commons hardwired supporting images were dropped pending verification.

records.edibility_detail: Edible, but mild. Best understood as a small northern woodland fruit rather than a major calorie species.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible, but mild. Best understood as a small northern woodland fruit rather than a major calorie species.

records.notes: Trailing or low bramble, very similar to blackberry, with aggregate fruits and prickly canes.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe fruits are eaten fresh or used like blackberries in jam, syrup, and desserts.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. This is best treated as a practical berry bucket near blackberry.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit. This is best treated as a practical berry bucket near blackberry.

use_links.notes: Broad bramble/dewberry context for this practical Rubus spp. fruit bucket.

records.notes: Robust grapevine with larger leaves and strongly flavored fruits.
records.culinary_uses: Strong-flavored grapes are good for juice, jelly, wine, and cooked fruit uses.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe grapes; many people prefer them processed.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe grapes; many people prefer them processed.

records.notes: Twining legume vine with pinnate leaves, maroon-brown pea flowers, and edible tubers along the roots.
records.culinary_uses: Protein-rich tubers are cooked like beans or potatoes and have major traditional food value.
records.edibility_detail: Edible tubers with real food value, though harvest takes patience.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: root / tuber / bulb. Edible tubers with real food value, though harvest takes patience.

records.source_note: Source links added for identification/range context; food-use wording remains modest and caution-framed where appropriate.

records.notes: Rough climbing bine with opposite to paired leaves and cone-like female flowers.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional sedative and calming herb, especially via cones.
records.culinary_uses: Young shoots can be eaten as a spring vegetable and cones are used for bitter aromatic flavoring.
records.edibility_detail: Shoots are the approachable edible use; cone use is more aromatic/herbal than snack food.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: stem / shoot / stalk, flower. Shoots are the approachable edible use; cone use is more aromatic/herbal than snack food.

records.notes: Low evergreen northern Vaccinium with tart red berries; kept separate from small cranberry because the two grow differently and should not share one silent image bucket.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly fruit use in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Tart red berries used for sauce, jam, syrup, and mixed preserves rather than casual handful eating.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, usually best cooked or sweetened.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit, usually best cooked or sweetened.

records.notes: Bog cranberry species with small tart red berries; split from lingonberry/lowbush cranberry so one image does not falsely represent both taxa.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly fruit use in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Tart berries can be used for sauce, preserves, syrup, and mixed fruit preparations when confidently identified and harvested legally.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe berries; usually best cooked or sweetened.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit, usually best cooked or sweetened.

records.notes: Wild river-edge grape with climbing habit and clustered dark fruits.
records.culinary_uses: Small tart grapes used in jelly, juice, wine, and cooked preserves.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, usually best processed.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit, usually best processed.

records.habitat: Moist acidic woods, mossy forest floor, hemlock/mixed woods.
records.edibility_detail: Berries are edible but small, bland, and low-volume; treat as a minor nibble, not a food source.
records.notes: Low creeping evergreen woodland plant with paired red berries. Included as a minor edible/woodland berry record, not a major food source.
records.general_notes: Low creeping evergreen woodland plant with paired red berries. Included as a minor edible/woodland berry record, not a major food source.
records.culinary_uses: Berries are edible but small, bland, and low-volume; treat as a minor nibble, not a food source.
records.medicinal_uses: No medicinal recommendation in this guide.
records.field_identification: Tiny trailing evergreen with opposite rounded leaves, paired flowers that form a red berry, and a low mat-like habit on forest floors.
records.plant_card_note: Tiny trailing evergreen with opposite rounded leaves, paired flowers that form a red berry, and a low mat-like habit on forest floors.

records.plant_lane_note: Berries / Fruits only; minor woodland nibble, not a bulk food.

records.notes: Low perennial with trifoliate toothed leaves, white five-petaled flowers, runners, and small red strawberries.
records.medicinal_uses: Leaf tea has traditional use, but this guide treats it mainly as a fruit and tea plant.
records.culinary_uses: Small ripe berries are eaten fresh or used like cultivated strawberries; leaves can be used for mild tea.
records.plant_lane_note: Fruit is the main part; flowers help ID the plant, and leaves are a secondary tea use.
records.plant_card_note: Small red fruits are the main prize; look for white flowers, trifoliate toothed leaves, and runners.
records.edibility_detail: Ripe fruit is edible. Leaves are a secondary tea use; avoid confusing with bland mock strawberry if the fruit/flower traits do not match.

records.notes: Low evergreen woodland plant with glossy leaves, white urn flowers, and red berry-like fruits.

records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic medicinal use exists; this guide treats that as historical/traditional context, not dosing guidance.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and berries are used in modest amounts for tea, candying, and flavoring rather than as bulk food.

records.edibility_detail: Edible in modest culinary use as tea, flavoring, or small fruit. Do not treat as medicinal dosing or bulk use.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry, leaf / tea. Edible in modest use as tea, flavoring, or small fruit; do not treat as medicinal dosing.

edible_use.method: Modest culinary tea / flavoring / small fruit
edible_use.notes: Leaves and berries are culinary flavoring/tea/small-fruit uses in modest amounts. Do not treat this as medicinal dosing.
